title: Share of land defined as natural habitat
subtitle: Natural ecosystems include forest, grassland, savanna, and shrublands, and other classes such as peatlands and marshes. It is total land minus land that is used by humans for agriculture – either croplands or pasture – or for infrastructure and urban environments.
caption:
Share of land defined as natural habitat
Natural ecosystems include forest, grassland, savanna, and shrublands, and other classes such as peatlands and marshes. It is total land minus land that is used by humans for agriculture – either croplands or pasture – or for infrastructure and urban environments.

This line chart tracks the percentage of total land area classified as natural habitat for the World, Germany, France, and the United Kingdom from 1780 to 2010. The vertical axis measures the share of land from 0% to 80%, while the horizontal axis spans the years 1780 through 2010. In 1780, the World line begins at the highest position, sitting near 75%, while the United Kingdom starts around 64%, France near 49%, and Germany at approximately 46%. Over the next century and a half, all four lines show a downward trajectory. The World line declines steadily to roughly 54% by 1950. The United Kingdom line drops more steeply, falling below 30% by 1950, marking the lowest point visible on the chart for that series. France and Germany also decline during this period, with France hovering between 40% and 45% and Germany dipping to roughly 40% by the mid-20th century. After 1950, the pattern shifts for the European nations. The lines for Germany, France, and the United Kingdom turn upward. Germany rises from its mid-century low to finish above 65% in 2010. France climbs to roughly 63%, and the United Kingdom recovers to just under 50%. In contrast, the World line continues a gradual descent after 1950, ending near 49% in 2010. By the end of the timeline, Germany and France occupy the highest positions on the chart, having surpassed their 1780 levels, while the global average sits lower than the starting point for the European countries but higher than the mid-century lows for the United Kingdom. The data shows a divergence where specific national shares of natural habitat increased in the latter half of the period while the global share continued to decrease.
